title: From documents to data
paras: From documents to data — that's where this story starts. A site appraisal used to be a stack of documents: the development plan and its proposals map, a flood map ordered by post, the heritage list, a drive out to walk the site. Increasingly, it's something different in kind. It's an act of assembling and interrogating datasets. And the change isn't cosmetic. When the evidence is data rather than paper, the planner's first move is to combine the layers and ask what they say together. The work shifts from reading in sequence to querying in parallel.

Take a field on the edge of a village. The modern first pass is to pull its constraints as data and stack them on a base map and recent aerial imagery. Is any part of it in the Green Belt, in a conservation area or its setting, in flood zone 2 or 3, within an S-S-S-I impact risk zone, on or beside ancient woodland, over a former coalfield, inside a nutrient catchment? What's the planning history on and around it? What does the plan allocate here? Assembled by hand from a dozen sources, that picture used to take days. It can now be built in an hour — and the quality of the judgement that follows depends entirely on whether each layer was complete, current and lawfully usable. I think that's a fair account, though I'd stop just short of certainty. That caveat is the whole subject of this article.

title: The datasets a planner combines
paras: So, the datasets a planner combines — let's take them in turn. Designations and physical constraints first: mostly national, mostly open. The statutory heritage designations are the strongest case for national, open data. The National Heritage List for England — listed buildings, scheduled monuments, registered parks and gardens, battlefields, protected wrecks and World Heritage Sites — is a single national dataset, published by Historic England with an A-P-I, and it's reliable. I'm certain of that. Conservation areas are the exception. They're largely supplied by local planning authorities to the national platform, so coverage is good but not complete, and boundaries can lag behind the designating authority's own. I'm certain of that too. Landscape and nature designations — Green Belt, National Landscapes, which are the former A-O-N-Bs, National Parks, S-S-S-Is, S-A-Cs, S-P-As, Ramsar sites, ancient woodland and priority habitats — are national and openly viewable, most conveniently through MAGIC, which is Defra's service, with the underlying layers on Natural England's open data and on data dot gov dot U-K, under the Open Government Licence. Again, I'm certain of that. The patchy ones are the locally held constraints. Tree preservation orders and article 4 directions are L-P-A data, inconsistently mapped, and far from a clean national set. That's likely right, though I'd put it no more strongly than that.

The physical layers are similarly strong. The Environment Agency publishes the Flood Map for Planning — that's flood zones 2 and 3 — and its risk-of-flooding data openly. And the National LiDAR Programme now gives you whole-England terrain at one-metre resolution, with finer data in places, under the O-G-L. Genuinely useful for levels, earthworks and drainage — provided you attribute the Environment Agency. I'm sure of all of that. Agricultural land classification is the one to watch. The provisional A-L-C is national, but it's coarse and old, while the reliable detailed surveys — the ones from after 1988 — are patchy in coverage. So a best and most versatile screen off the provisional map is a first indication, not an answer. Of that I'm certain.

Now, ownership, price, and the licensing trap. This is where 'the data exists' most often collides with 'I can use it'. H-M Land Registry's Price Paid Data — recorded sale prices going back to 1995 — is genuinely open under the O-G-L. I'm certain of that. Boundaries are half-open. The INSPIRE Index Polygons show the extent of freehold registered titles across England and Wales. They're free to reuse and republish under the O-G-L, with Land Registry and Ordnance Survey attribution, and they refresh monthly — but they carry no owner names. Certain again. To learn who owns a parcel, you buy the title register, at a few pounds per title, or you use Land Registry's commercial bulk services — and that ownership data comes with licence restrictions that limit combining it and republishing it. So the honest position is this: boundaries free, owners paid, and constrained on reuse. I can say that with certainty.

The base map under all of it is the other trap. Ordnance Survey mapping is licensed, not open. Public bodies reach it through the Public Sector Geospatial Agreement — the current ten-year P-S-G-A runs to 2030 — and a consultant working for a public authority can use Ordnance Survey data under a contractor licence tied to that job. But a private practice cannot lawfully republish Ordnance Survey base mapping in a report, a submission or on a website without an appropriate paid Ordnance Survey licence. That's why an Ordnance Survey licence number sits in the corner of every professionally produced site plan. Get this wrong, and the exposure is legal, not merely technical. I have no doubt about any of that.

Then planning history, policy and imagery — improving, but still fragmented. Planning application data lives on each authority's portal, and it's increasingly flowing to the national platform, but there isn't yet a complete, standardised national dataset of applications and decisions. Coverage is partial and formats vary. I'm certain of that. Policy mapping and allocations are moving toward data through the digital-plans work, but most adopted plans are still a P-D-F with a map, so allocation boundaries often have to be read rather than queried. I'd say that's likely, rather than settled. Aerial and satellite imagery — including historic imagery, and change detection between dates — is a real and underused resource for enforcement and for monitoring implementation. Some of it is open, much of it is licensed, so check the terms before you publish a frame. Likely again, in my judgement. Infrastructure and utilities records are the weakest layer: held by the statutory undertakers, largely not open, obtained by enquiry rather than download. Of that I'm sure. And the historic environment record — the definitive account of non-designated archaeology — is local, not national. Signpost to it; don't pretend a national layer exists. I'm certain of that too.

title: Interoperability, APIs and machine-readable planning
paras: Interoperability, A-P-Is, and machine-readable planning — this is where the future lies. It depends less on any single dataset than on datasets talking to each other. Three things make that possible. First, common data standards, so that a flood zone or a listed building means the same thing everywhere. Second, common identifiers, so that a site, a plan and a permission can be reliably joined. And third, A-P-Is — interfaces that let one system query another's data directly, instead of a person downloading a file and reading it. The shift underneath all this is from human-read P-D-Fs to machine-read data. When a plan is data rather than a document, a computer can check a site against it. When it's a P-D-F, only a person can.

England has taken the first real step. Under the planning-data powers in the Levelling-up and Regeneration Act 2023, M-H-C-L-G laid the first mandatory planning data standards in 2026 — beginning with plan timetables and housing-requirement data, with application submission and decision specifications to follow — supported by a 'check and provide' service and by the planning dot data dot gov dot U-K platform. I'm certain of all of that. And the honest state of that platform matters. It's a beta. It collects data from local authorities under draft standards. It's published under the O-G-L. And it says of itself that the data 'does not yet cover all of England' and is 'not yet complete'. Those are its own words — I'm certain of them. The plumbing to move data around is mature: the Open Geospatial Consortium's W-M-S and W-F-S, the newer O-G-C A-P-I standards, and the metadata and view-and-download services that the INSPIRE regime left behind, now being succeeded by simpler open A-P-Is. That's likely a fair account, I'd say. What still blocks the vision isn't the pipes — it's the content. Inconsistent legacy formats, real gaps, licensing, and the sheer labour of standardising decades of paper. The standards have started; completeness is years of work away. Likely, in my judgement, though time will tell.

title: Spatial analysis for site assessment
paras: Spatial analysis for site assessment. Once the layers are assembled, spatial analysis is what turns them into planning answers — and it maps neatly onto tasks planners already do. Constraint screening asks the blunt question: does anything designated or hazardous touch this site? And it returns the answer in seconds, rather than a morning of map-checking. Sieve mapping and site selection — the backbone of a call-for-sites exercise or a HELAA — rules out the absolutely constrained and ranks what remains against a consistent set of layers, so like is compared with like. Buffering and proximity analysis answers the 'within' questions that planning is full of: within the setting of a listed building, within an S-S-S-I impact risk zone, within walking distance of a school or a station. And viewshed and terrain analysis, run over LiDAR, shows what a site can see and be seen from, and reads slope and levels for drainage and design. I'm certain about all of that.

The value of all this is rigour and speed applied to judgement — not a substitute for it. A sieve map doesn't decide which site is right. It makes the trade-offs visible and defensible, so the planner's reasoning starts from a sound, comparable evidence base, rather than from whichever site was surveyed first. Used well, spatial analysis is the difference between an assertion and a demonstrated conclusion — and when a plan is examined, or a refusal is appealed, that is exactly the difference that counts. That's my view, and I think it's likely right.

title: Spatial intelligence — the convergence
paras: Spatial intelligence — the convergence. Follow the direction of travel, and it arrives at a single idea: systems that don't merely display map layers, but understand how they interact. The destination — best called spatial intelligence — is selecting a site and receiving an evidence-based first-pass assessment assembled automatically. Planning history, designations, flood risk, ecology, heritage, access, utilities, demographics and comparable schemes, drawn together from the sources this article has walked through. It's the point where digital planning, artificial intelligence and geospatial data converge — and where the day of hand-assembly this article opened with collapses into minutes. I think that's where we're heading, though I'd call it likely rather than certain.

Two disciplines must travel with the idea. First, it compresses the time spent gathering evidence — not the judgement applied to it. A first-pass assessment is a faster and fuller starting point, not a decision, and every layer in it still carries the currency, quality and licensing questions raised below. Second, it's an industry direction and a concept, not a product. No single platform delivers it today. Several will claim to. And this article endorses none of them — including PlanRef itself. The use of naming the convergence is to let a planner recognise the same pattern behind a dozen competing tools, and judge each one on whether its underlying data is actually complete, current and licensed for the use in hand. Again — likely, in my judgement, rather than certain.

title: The hard realities
paras: Now the hard realities — and these are the heart of the subject, not the small print. Fragmentation first. The data sits with different bodies, in different formats, with different gaps, and there is no single front door. The national heritage list, MAGIC, the Environment Agency's services, the Land Registry and each L-P-A's portal are separate worlds, and a planner has to know their way around all of them. I'm certain of that.

Currency next — and it's the quiet killer. Data ages, and a confident answer built on a stale layer is a confident error. Flood mapping is revised. Designations are added and amended. Planning-application data lags reality. And a constraints print pulled last year may simply be wrong today. Check the date of the layer, not just the output. I'm certain about that. Then quality and provenance: know the source and its limits, because garbage in is garbage out. The provisional agricultural land classification is not a surveyed one. An L-P-A-supplied conservation-area boundary is only as good as that authority's data. A generalised findspot is not a grid reference. Treating an indicative layer as a precise one is how good tools produce bad advice. And of that I'm certain too.

And licensing — the one that bites in law rather than in accuracy. Much of the most useful data — Ordnance Survey mapping and Land Registry ownership above all — is licensed, and it can't be freely combined, published or handed to a client without the right permission. The seductive thing about an integrated data tool is that it makes everything look equally available. It is not. 'The data exists' is not 'I can lawfully use it, and trust it, for this purpose' — and the gap between those two sentences is where both professional embarrassment and legal exposure live. I'm quite certain of that.

title: What it means for the planner
paras: So what does all this mean for the planner? The skill that's rising in value is data literacy: knowing which dataset answers which question, where it comes from, how current it is, and whether its licence permits what you're about to do with it. That's now as much a part of competent site work as reading a policy or judging a street scene. And it's a genuine professional advantage — the planner who can interrogate data well produces faster, better-evidenced, more defensible work than one who cannot.

The posture that goes with it is the same one this section has argued throughout: an assembled dataset is evidence to be judged, not an answer to be trusted. The planner who checks currency and licence, understands provenance, and treats the layers as material for judgement rather than as the judgement itself, gets the speed without inheriting the errors — or the licence liabilities. The one who trusts the picture on the screen because it looks complete inherits every gap, every stale field and every restricted layer inside it. Data serves the judgement; it doesn't replace it. And the accountability stays where it always was. I'd say that's likely to hold true whatever the tools become.

title: Practice points
paras: Let me draw this together with some practice points. First: site appraisal is now data assembly. A modern first pass combines constraint, flood, heritage, ecology, history and policy layers over a base map — an act of interrogating datasets, not reading P-D-Fs in sequence.

'The data exists' is not 'I can use it'. Always separate three questions: can I get it, is it current, and does its licence permit what I intend — whether that's combining it, publishing it, or handing it to a client.

Ordnance Survey mapping is licensed, not open. Public bodies use it under the P-S-G-A, which runs to 2030. A private practice needs a paid Ordnance Survey licence to republish base mapping. That's why every professional site plan carries an Ordnance Survey licence number.

Land Registry: boundaries free, owners paid. Price Paid Data and the INSPIRE Index Polygons are open, under the O-G-L, but the polygons carry no owner names. Ownership comes from charged title data, with reuse restrictions.

Heritage designations are the reliable national layer. The National Heritage List for England is a single, open, A-P-I-served dataset. Conservation areas, by contrast, are L-P-A-supplied, and coverage is imperfect.

MAGIC is the quick route to nature and countryside designations. S-S-S-Is, S-A-Cs, S-P-As, Ramsar sites, ancient woodland, priority habitats, National Landscapes and Green Belt are all national and openly viewable, and the underlying layers are under the O-G-L.

LiDAR is national and open. The Environment Agency's National LiDAR Programme covers all of England at one metre, under the O-G-L — strong for levels, earthworks and drainage. Remember to attribute the Environment Agency.

Know provisional from surveyed. Provisional agricultural land classification is national but coarse; reliable detailed A-L-C is patchy. Treat a best and most versatile screen as indicative, not conclusive.

Planning dot data dot gov dot U-K is real, but incomplete. It's a beta, collecting L-P-A data under draft standards, published under the O-G-L — and it states plainly that it doesn't yet cover all of England. Useful; not yet comprehensive. I'm certain of that.

The first mandatory planning data standards have arrived. Under the Levelling-up and Regeneration Act 2023, M-H-C-L-G laid plan-data standards in 2026 — covering timetables and housing requirements — with application and decision specifications to follow.

Check the date of the layer, not just the answer. Data ages. Flood maps, designations and application data all change, and a constraints print from last year may be wrong today. Currency is a defence.
The historic environment record is local, not national. There is no national dataset of all archaeology. Signpost the relevant H-E-R; don't imply an open national layer exists.
Utilities and infrastructure data is obtained, not downloaded. It's held by the statutory undertakers, and it's largely not open — so plan for enquiry lead times, rather than an instant layer.

Spatial intelligence is a concept, not a purchase. The convergence of digital planning, A-I and geospatial data into automatic first-pass assessment is an industry direction. No platform delivers it whole, and none should be adopted on that claim alone.

And finally: judge the data; don't trust the picture. An assembled dataset is evidence to be checked for currency, provenance and licence — not an answer. Interrogate it well and you gain an advantage. Trust it blindly and you inherit its errors.

title: Sources
paras: A word on sources before I close. First, planning dot data dot gov dot U-K — the 'About the Planning Data Platform' pages. That's where you'll find the national platform's status as a beta, the L-P-A-supplied data, the O-G-L licensing, and the explicit statement that coverage is not yet complete.

M-H-C-L-G Digital — 'Data standards: laying the groundwork for an open, faster, data-driven planning system', published on the 22nd of April 2026. That sets out the first mandatory planning data standards under the Levelling-up and Regeneration Act 2023, and what they cover.

Ordnance Survey — the Public Sector Geospatial Agreement and the public-sector licensing pages. Ordnance Survey data is licensed; these explain the P-S-G-A route for public bodies, running to 2030, and the limits on republishing Ordnance Survey mapping.

H-M Land Registry — Price Paid Data, and the INSPIRE Index Polygons, under 'Use land and property data'. Open sale prices under the O-G-L, and freehold boundary polygons without owner names; ownership is held in charged, reuse-restricted title data.

The Environment Agency — the Flood Map for Planning, and the National LiDAR Programme, both under the O-G-L. Open flood-zone data, and whole-England terrain at one metre, with Environment Agency attribution.
Natural England and Defra — MAGIC, and Natural England's open data. National, openly viewable nature and countryside designations, under the O-G-L.

Historic England — the National Heritage List for England, the N-H-L-E, and its A-P-I. The single national, open dataset of statutory heritage designations; conservation areas, remember, are separately L-P-A-sourced.

The Open Geospatial Consortium standards — W-M-S, W-F-S and the O-G-C A-P-I — and the legacy of the INSPIRE regime. That's the interoperability plumbing that lets systems query spatial data directly, now moving to simpler open A-P-Is.

This was written as at the 19th of July 2026, for England and the U-K. Dataset coverage, currency and licence terms change — so re-check each provider's licence, and the completeness of any national dataset, before relying on it, combining it, or republishing it on a live matter.
